elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we present a technique for repairing data race errors in parallel programs written in C/C++ and Fortran using the OpenMP API.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our technique can also remove barriers that are deemed unnecessary for correctness.
elements.sent-2411-003: We implement these ideas in our tool called \tool, which takes a language-independent approach to provide appropriate placements of synchronization constructs to avoid data races.
elements.sent-2411-004: To the best of our knowledge, \tool is the only tool that can repair parallel programs that use the OpenMP API.
elements.sent-2411-005: We showcase the capabilities of \tool by performing extensive experiments on $415$ parallel programs.
elements.sent-2411-006: Programs that can solve problems using multiple threads in parallel are defined as parallel programs.
elements.sent-2411-007: Parallelism can be achieved through concurrent computing using threads on CPUs or GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-008: There are two common approaches for achieving parallelism: Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data (MIMD) and Single Instruction, Multiple Data (SIMD).
elements.sent-2411-009: The OpenMP API~\cite{dagum1998openmp provides a cross-platform abstraction for programs written in C/C++ and Fortran for both of these approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-010: A data race occurs in a parallel program when two or more threads are accessing the same memory location at the same time, and at least one of those accesses is a write.
elements.sent-2411-011: The behavior of such programs is unpredictable.
elements.sent-2411-012: Data races are one of the frequently encountered issues in concurrent computing.
elements.sent-2411-013: There is a substantial positive financial impact in identifying and repairing these errors early in the development cycle~\cite{boehm1988understanding .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The data race exists because of the parallel reading and writing of the shared array \TT{data .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Placing a barrier (\TT{\#pragma omp barrier ) in the program at line \ref{codeline:race:barrier mitigates the data race by ensuring that all the threads reach it before any of them can proceed further.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: In this example, the data race is because of the \TT{parallel for loop.}
elements.sent-2411-017: OpenMP does not support barrier constructs inside a \TT{parallel for loop.}
elements.sent-2411-018: The data race can be avoided by adding the ordered clause to the \TT{for loop (line \ref{codeline:race:ordered_for ) and placing the statements causing the data race inside an ordered region (\TT{\#pragma omp ordered ), as shown in line \ref{codeline:race:ordered .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Note that there can be multiple statements in this ordered region.
elements.lstlisting-2411-001: [caption=This OpenMP program without the highlighted line contains a data race, label={Cd:race}, captionpos=b, xleftmargin=2em, escapechar=\^] int data[NUM_THREADS+1]; #pragma omp parallel { int id = omp_get_thread_num(); int temp = data[id+1]; ^\label{Line:in:read}^ ^\highlightcode{0.35}^#pragma omp barrier ^\label{codeline:race:barrier}^ data[id] = temp; ^\label{Line:in:write}^ }
elements.lstlisting-2411-002: [caption=This OpenMP program without the highlighted snippets contains a data race inside the \TT{for} loop, label={Cd:race_for}, captionpos=b, xleftmargin=2em, escapechar=\^] int data[count+1]; #pragma omp parallel for ^\highlightcode{0.13}^ordered^\label{codeline:race:ordered_for}^ for (int i=0; i<count; i++) { int temp = data[i+1]; ^\label{Line:in:read2}^ ^\highlightcode{0.35}^#pragma omp ordered ^\label{codeline:race:ordered}^ data[i] = temp; ^\label{Line:in:write2}^ }
elements.sent-2411-020: In this tool paper, we make the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-021: We introduce our tool, \tool, which takes a language-independent approach to automatically fix data race errors in programs written using the OpenMP API.
elements.sent-2411-022: For OpenMP programs that contain \TT{parallel regions, our paper proposes a technique to provide barrier placements to avoid data races.}
elements.sent-2411-023: For OpenMP programs that contain \TT{parallel for loops, our paper proposes a technique to identify the statements that need to be placed in an ordered region to avoid data races.}
elements.sent-2411-024: In addition to the above two techniques that introduce synchronization constructs to avoid data races, our paper proposes a methodology to remove existing barriers and ordered regions inserted by the programmer if deemed unnecessary.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our paper showcases the practical differences between the two different solver strategies (\mhs and \maxsat) that are employed during the repair process.
elements.sent-2411-026: We showcase the effectiveness of our tool by performing extensive experimental evaluation on $415$ parallel programs that use the OpenMP API.
elements.sent-2411-027: This benchmark set consists of $235$ C/C++ programs and $180$ Fortran programs, highlighting the language versatility of \tool.
elements.sent-2411-028: To the best of our knowledge, ours is the only technique and tool that can propose a fix for parallel programs written using the OpenMP API.
elements.sent-2411-029: In \sectref{llor , we describe the working of \tool in fine detail, discuss related work in \sectref{relatedwork , and in \sectref{experiments , we provide a detailed background on the experimental setup and present the results summary of running \tool against the benchmark suite.}}}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{The Repair Algorithm} \label{Alg:llor} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE \emp{Input:} Instrumented Program $P$ \STATE \emp{Output:} Repaired Program $P_{sol}$ \STATE $\mathit{\varphi := true}$ \label{algte:constraint} \LOOP \label{algte:startloop} \STATE $\mathit{\inangleb{res,sol} := Solve(\varphi)}$ \label{algte:solve} \IF {$\mathit{res = UNSAT}$} \label{algte:unsat} \PRINT \emp{Error:} Program cannot be repaired \label{algte:unsaterror} \RETURN \emp{errorcode} \ENDIF \STATE $\mathit{P_{sol} := GenerateRepairCandidate(P,sol)}$ \label{algte:repaircandidate} \STATE $\mathit{\inangleb{result, \pi} := Verify(P_{sol})}$ \label{algte:verify} \IF {$\mathit{result = SAFE}$} \label{algte:repaired} \STATE \emp{break} \ENDIF \IF {$\mathit{result \neq RACE}$} \label{algte:asserterror} \PRINT \emp{Error:} Program cannot be repaired \RETURN \emp{errorcode} \ENDIF \STATE $\mathit{c := GenerateClause(\pi)}$ \label{algte:generateclause} \STATE $\mathit{\varphi := \varphi \cup \{c\}}$ \label{algte:addclause} \ENDLOOP \label{algte:endloop} \RETURN $P_{sol}$ \label{algte:returnsol} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-030: Identifying data races in parallel programs has been an active area of research for quite a while.
elements.sent-2411-031: Some of the early techniques proposed in this area were based on the \EM{happens-before relation defined by Lamport~\cite{lamport2019time .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Mellor-Crummey~\cite{mellor1991fly introduced a new protocol that uses Offset-Span Labeling of the nodes in a fork-join graph that represents the concurrency relationships among threads in an execution of a fork-join program.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In addition to these, several other techniques~\cite{dinning1991detecting,netzer1991race,perkovic1996online,joshi2012new have been introduced to detect races in parallel programs.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Tools like \app{GPUVerify ~\cite{betts2012gpuverify,betts2015design , \app{ESBMC-GPU ~\cite{monteiro2018esbmc , \app{VerCors ~\cite{blom2014specification,amighi2015specification , \app{PUG ~\cite{li2010scalable , and \app{GKLEE ~\cite{li2012gklee propose techniques to detect data races in GPU programs.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Tools like \app{ompVerify ~\cite{basupalli2011ompverify , \app{DRACO ~\cite{ye2018using , \app{PolyOMP ~\cite{chatarasi2016static,chatarasi2017extended , \app{SWORD ~\cite{atzeni2018sword , \app{OMPT ~\cite{eichenberger2013ompt , \app{Helgrind ~\cite{valgrind2007helgrind , and \app{LLOV ~\cite{bora2020llov have been introduced to identify data races in programs using the OpenMP API.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Some of these tools~\cite{basupalli2011ompverify,ye2018using,chatarasi2016static,chatarasi2017extended,bora2020llov use only static techniques, while some~\cite{atzeni2018sword,eichenberger2013ompt,valgrind2007helgrind employ both static and dynamic techniques.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Using automatic techniques to repair bugs in programs has been another area of research that aligns closely with our work.
elements.sent-2411-038: The research works in this area can be broadly classified into techniques that attempt to repair sequential programs and techniques that attempt to repair concurrent programs.
elements.sent-2411-039: The approaches that focus on sequential programs~\cite{jobstmann2005program,griesmayer2006repair,chandra2011angelic,malik2011constraint take a set of assertions that need to be met and an input program that fails one or more of these assertions.}
elements.sent-2411-040: The technique then modifies a small subset of statements in the input program to ensure that all the provided assertions pass.
elements.sent-2411-041: The approaches that focus on concurrent programs~\cite{vechev2010abstraction,muzahid2010atomtracker,deshmukh2010logical,vcerny2011quantitative,jin2011automated,joshi2014automatically,joshi2015property,joshi2021gpurepair work on an input program that passes all the assertions when run sequentially but has inconsistent behavior when run in parallel using multiple threads.}
elements.sent-2411-042: This inconsistent behavior is because of the interleaving of threads.
elements.sent-2411-043: Repairing such programs involves introducing critical regions, locks, and problem-specific synchronization constructs.
elements.sent-2411-044: In this paper, we instrument the given concurrent input program with the possible locations of the synchronization constructs and generate repair candidates based on the error traces obtained from the verifier.
elements.sent-2411-045: Similar techniques~\cite{joshi2014automatically,joshi2015property,joshi2021gpurepair have been used in the past to repair concurrent programs.}
elements.sent-2411-046: To the best of our knowledge, \tool is the only tool that can fix data races in OpenMP programs written in C/C++ and Fortran.
elements.sent-2411-047: The counter-example driven repair technique used in this paper is similar to one of our earlier works~\cite{joshi2021gpurepair that focuses on the automated repair of GPU kernels and the implementation of the technique in our tool, \gpurepair.}
elements.sent-2411-048: However, the motivations behind \gpurepair and \tool are fundamentally different, causing the repair algorithm to differ in a non-trivial fashion.
elements.sent-2411-049: GPU kernels work on the principle of Single Instruction Multiple Data (SIMD).
elements.sent-2411-050: The threads created in the kernel are organized as blocks.
elements.sent-2411-052: The threads within a warp generally execute in a lock-step manner.
elements.sent-2411-053: There could be data races within the threads of a block.
elements.sent-2411-054: Data races in GPU kernels are avoided using barrier constructs.
elements.sent-2411-055: However, there might be a scenario where some of the threads in a block reach the barrier, and some do not, resulting in a deadlock.
elements.sent-2411-056: This problem is called barrier divergence.
elements.sent-2411-057: Since OpenMP programs with these constructs work on the principle of Multiple Instruction Multiple Data (MIMD), the problem of barrier divergence does not exist here.
elements.sent-2411-058: Besides proposing barrier placements for \TT{omp parallel regions, our technique also identifies the statements that need to be placed within ordered regions for \TT{omp parallel for loops.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Since \gpurepair has to repair errors due to data race and barrier divergence errors, the repair algorithm has to evaluate which barriers need to be removed and which barriers need to be added.
elements.sent-2411-060: The algorithm achieves this by generating positive monotone clauses for data race errors and negative monotone clauses for barrier divergence errors.
elements.sent-2411-061: However, in the case of \tool, the repair algorithm generates only positive monotone clauses.
elements.sent-2411-062: The instrumentation component of \tool instruments the LLVM IR generated from the OpenMP programs.
elements.sent-2411-063: On the other hand, \gpurepair uses Boogie~\cite{barnett2005boogie as an intermediate representation which is generated from GPU kernels.}
elements.sent-2411-064: These differences give rise to an entirely different implementation stack, with \tool using C++ while \gpurepair uses C\#.
elements.sent-2411-065: Another notable difference in \tool is that we do not add conditional barriers but instead add metadata on possible locations of barriers.
elements.sent-2411-066: In this section, we present the experimental results of running \tool on various C/C++ and Fortran benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-067: The source code of \tool is available at \cite{llorsrc .}
elements.sent-2411-068: The artifacts used to reproduce the results of this paper are available at \cite{vmcaiartifact .}
elements.sent-2411-069: Several tools are involved in the pipeline of \tool, as introduced in \sectref{workflow .}
elements.sent-2411-070: The instrumentation component is developed as an LLVM pass and is therefore built in C++.
elements.sent-2411-071: The repair component is outside the LLVM infrastructure and is built using the .NET Framework with C\# as the programming language.
elements.sent-2411-072: We use the \zth solver~\cite{de2008z3,bjorner2015nuz to solve the constraints generated during the repair phase.}
elements.sent-2411-073: The tools used in \tool and their versions are: \href{https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/tree/release/12.x/llvm {\app{LLVM 12.0.0 , \href{https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/tree/release/12.x/clang {\app{Clang 12.0.0 , \href{https://github.com/flang-compiler/flang {\app{Flang 12.0.1 , and \href{https://github.com/utpalbora/LLOV/tree/llov-v0.3 {\verifier 0.3 .}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The experiments were performed on \emp{Standard\_F2s\_v2 Azure\textsuperscript{\textregistered virtual machine , which has 2 vCPUs and 4 GiB of memory.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: More details on the virtual machine can be found at \cite{azuref2sv2 .}
elements.sent-2411-076: A total of $415$ programs ($235$ C/C++ and $180$ Fortran) were considered for the evaluation of \tool.
elements.sent-2411-077: This benchmark set consists of programs from the DataRaceBench~\cite{liao2017dataracebench test suite, Exascale~\cite{exascale project, Rodinia~\cite{che2009rodinia test suite, and Parallel Research Kernels~\cite{parres , along with additional benchmarks introduced while developing \tool.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The benchmarks from DataRaceBench and \tool test suites have one source file per benchmark, and the remaining have multiple source files per benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-079: For benchmarks that have a single source file, the experiments were performed with a timeout of $300$ seconds for each benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-080: For benchmarks that have multiple source files, the experiments were performed with a timeout of $60$ seconds for each source file and an overall timeout of $1800$ seconds for the benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-081: Each benchmark has been executed $3$ times, and the average time of these $3$ runs is taken into consideration.
elements.sent-2411-082: We used the average since there was a negligible difference between the median and the average.
elements.figure-2411-001: \caption{Benchmark Summary} \label{Ta:benchmark_summary} \begin{center} \def\arraystretch{1.1} \setlength\tabcolsep{7pt} \begin{tabular}{|l|r|r|r|} \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{Source} \multicolumn{3}{|c|}{Programs} \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{} \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{C/C++} \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{Fortran} \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{Total} DataRaceBench $181$ $168$ $349$ Exascale Project $8$ $0$ $8$ Rodinia $18$ $0$ $18$ Parallel Research Kernels $11$ $0$ $11$ Other Large Benchmarks $5$ $0$ $5$ \toolname Test Suite $12$ $12$ $24$ \end{tabular} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-083: The results obtained from running \tool against the benchmark suite are summarized in \tableref{results .}
elements.sent-2411-084: The table categorizes the results into three categories based on the output of \verifier.
elements.sent-2411-085: The \hyperlink{racefree {first category includes all the programs for which \verifier concluded that there were no errors.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: On manual inspection, we found out that for some of the Fortran programs (e.g., \path{baseline_fortran/B01_simple_race.f95 , \path{dataracebench_fortran/DRB002-antidep1-var-yes.f95 ), \verifier was not detecting a data race, even if one existed.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Since the soundness and completeness guarantees of \tool are modulo the completeness of the verifier, \tool could not propose a fix for these programs.
elements.sent-2411-088: On the other hand, because of this behavior, \tool suggests removing existing barriers or ordered regions in some Fortran programs even though they are necessary.
elements.sent-2411-089: Note that this behavior of \verifier is limited to Fortran programs, and we did not come across any C/C++ programs that were impacted by this.
elements.sent-2411-090: For \hyperlink{racefree_changes {$11$ programs in this category, \tool recommended changes.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: For the remaining $8$ programs, \tool was correct in suggesting the removal of barriers and ordered regions.
elements.sent-2411-092: The \hyperlink{racedetected {second category includes the programs for which \verifier had identified data races.}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Out of the $147$ programs in this category, \tool was able to fix \hyperlink{repaired {$107$ programs correctly.}}
elements.sent-2411-094: Upon manual inspection, we found that $11$ of these were similar, where an integer value was incremented through a static pointer variable.
elements.sent-2411-095: In the LLVM IR code, the address of the static pointer was copied to local variables, and the value was updated through these local variables.
elements.sent-2411-096: The instrumentation component of \tool could not track these operations, causing it to not instrument instructions that should have been instrumented.
elements.sent-2411-097: No placement of barriers can fix such programs.
elements.sent-2411-098: The remaining $3$ programs had complex OpenMP constructs like \EM{teams and \EM{target , because of which simple barrier placements could not fix the data races.}}
elements.sent-2411-099: These programs are beyond the scope of \tool since barriers and ordered regions cannot fix data races in these programs.
elements.figure-2411-002: \caption{Count of programs grouped by category} \label{Ta:results} \begin{center} \def\arraystretch{1.1} \setlength\tabcolsep{7pt} \begin{tabular}{|l|r|r|} \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{Source} \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{C/C++} \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{Fortran} \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{Total Benchmarks} $235$ $180$ \hypertarget{racefree} I. No data races identified by \verifier $75$ $129$ \quad No changes made by \tool $71$ $122$ \hypertarget{racefree_changes} \quad Changes recommended by \tool $4$ $7$ \hypertarget{racedetected} II. Data races identified by \verifier $117$ $30$ \hypertarget{repaired} \quad Repaired by \tool $92$ $15$ \hypertarget{repairerror} \quad Could not be repaired by \tool $5$ $11$ \hypertarget{timedout} \quad Timeouts $9$ $0$ \hypertarget{unsupported} \quad Unsupported $11$ $4$ \hypertarget{llovunsupported} III. Unsupported by \verifier $43$ $21$ \hypertarget{llorunsupported} \quad Unsupported by \tool $37$ $11$ \hypertarget{compileerror} \quad Compilation errors $0$ $10$ \hypertarget{verifyerror} \quad Verification errors $6$ $0$ \end{tabular} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-100: The \hyperlink{llovunsupported {final category includes the programs that are either unsupported by \verifier or failed compilation.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: There are $64$ programs in this category.
elements.sent-2411-102: The \app{flang version that we used for experimentation threw compilation errors for \hyperlink{compileerror {$10$ Fortran programs and did not generate \llvm IR.}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: Because of that, we could not attempt to repair them.
elements.sent-2411-104: Since \tool depends on \verifier in the repair process, it could not generate any repair candidates for \hyperlink{llorunsupported {$48$ programs .}}
elements.sent-2411-105: As mentioned in \sectref{repair_algorithm , \tool offers two different ways of solving the clauses generated from the error traces.}
elements.sent-2411-106: The default approach uses the minimal-hitting-set (\mhs) strategy.
elements.sent-2411-107: The user can switch to the \maxsat strategy if they wish to.
elements.sent-2411-108: The \maxsat strategy is computationally heavier than the \mhs strategy since the \mhs solver uses a polynomial time algorithm, whereas the \maxsat solver makes multiple queries to the SAT solver.
elements.sent-2411-109: In most practical cases, the \mhs strategy would provide a similar solution to the \maxsat strategy.
elements.sent-2411-110: Because of this, the \mhs strategy has a performance advantage over the \maxsat strategy.
elements.sent-2411-112: The \mhs solver could choose $a$ to be the solution, and the \maxsat solver could choose $b$ to be the solution.
elements.sent-2411-113: Both of the solutions are valid for the clause, but choosing $b$ could fix the program, and choosing $a$ may not, thus forcing more iterations.
elements.sent-2411-114: Because of these reasons, we notice that for some benchmarks, the \mhs strategy performs better, and the \maxsat strategy performs better for others.
elements.sent-2411-115: Out of the $415$ benchmarks, only $133$ benchmarks did not result in a timeout and needed the use of a solver strategy (either \mhs or \maxsat).
elements.sent-2411-116: In $93$ of these benchmarks, the \mhs strategy performed better than the \maxsat strategy.
elements.sent-2411-117: Most of the benchmarks executed within a second, so \figref{solver_time is limited to $600$ milliseconds on the x-axis and y-axis to showcase those benchmarks.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Any benchmark that took more than $600$ milliseconds has been plotted on the top-right corner of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-119: Additional experiments and analysis are included in \ref{Apx:results , which provides more experimental details like the code size of the benchmarks (\ref{Apx:codesize ), case studies (\ref{Apx:examples ), and how to run \tool (\ref{Apx:running ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: In this tool paper, we introduce \tool, which can fix data race errors in OpenMP programs written in C/C++ and Fortran.
elements.sent-2411-121: We have affirmed the effectiveness of our work by attempting repair on multiple benchmark suites (consisting of 415 C/C++ and Fortran programs) using our tool.
elements.sent-2411-122: The technique behind \tool can support any OpenMP verifier.
elements.sent-2411-123: Leveraging other static and dynamic verifiers might help repair some of the programs that the combination of \tool and \verifier could not.
elements.sent-2411-124: More details on these verifiers have been provided in \ref{Apx:verifiers .}
elements.sent-2411-125: We thank the Ministry of Education, India, for their financial support.
elements.sent-2411-126: The average number of lines of code for the test suite is $694.92$, and the median is $44$.
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{minipage}{.45\textwidth} \begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.7] \selectcolormodel{gray} \begin{axis}[ xbar, xmin=0, xmax=250, xlabel={Kernel Count}, symbolic y coords={ {$> 100$}, {$81-100$}, {$61-80$}, {$41-60$}, {$21-40$}, {$<= 20$} }, ytick=data, ylabel={Lines of Code}, nodes near coords, nodes near coords align={horizontal}, height=200pt, width=200pt ] \addplot coordinates { (58,{$> 100$}) (5,{$81-100$}) (67,{$61-80$}) (87,{$41-60$}) (177,{$21-40$}) (21,{$<= 20$}) }; \end{axis} \end{tikzpicture} \caption{Lines of Code} \label{Fi:code_lines} \end{minipage} \hfil \begin{minipage}{.5\textwidth} \begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.7] \selectcolormodel{gray} \begin{axis}[ xbar, xmin=0, xmax=250, xlabel={Kernel Count}, symbolic y coords={ {$> 400$}, {$301-400$}, {$201-300$}, {$101-200$}, {$51-100$}, {$<= 50$} }, ytick=data, ylabel={LLVM IR Instructions}, nodes near coords, nodes near coords align={horizontal}, height=200pt, width=200pt ] \addplot coordinates { (78,{$> 400$}) (27,{$301-400$}) (38,{$201-300$}) (88,{$101-200$}) (123,{$51-100$}) (51,{$<= 50$}) }; \end{axis} \end{tikzpicture} \caption{LLVM IR Instructions} \label{Fi:llvm_insts} \end{minipage}
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.7] \selectcolormodel{gray} \begin{axis}[ xbar, xmin=0, xmax=250, xlabel={Kernel Count}, symbolic y coords={ {$> 30$}, {$11-30$}, {$6-10$}, {$4-5$}, {$1-3$}, {$0$} }, ytick=data, ylabel={Barrier Variables}, nodes near coords, nodes near coords align={horizontal}, height=200pt, width=200pt ] \addplot coordinates { (37,{$> 30$}) (14,{$11-30$}) (33,{$6-10$}) (69,{$4-5$}) (101,{$1-3$}) (151,{$0$}) }; \end{axis} \end{tikzpicture} \caption{Barrier Variables} \label{Fi:barrier_variables}
elements.sent-2411-127: The instrumentation component of \tool works with LLVM IR and not on the source code.
elements.sent-2411-128: A line of source code could result in zero (e.g., code comments) or more LLVM IR instructions.
elements.sent-2411-129: LLVM IR was generated for $405$ programs.
elements.sent-2411-130: The average number of LLVM IR instructions for the test suite is $1995.34$, and the median is $115$.
elements.sent-2411-131: The repair step of \tool depends on the number of barrier variables that have been introduced during instrumentation.
elements.sent-2411-132: The average number of barrier variables for the test suite is $71.01$, and the median is $2$.
elements.sent-2411-133: More than $50\%$ of the programs had less than 3 barrier variables.
elements.sent-2411-134: In this section, we go over some of the programs that caused either \verifier or \tool to fail.
elements.sent-2411-136: Multiple threads are incrementing the value in the address pointed by the static pointer variable, thereby causing a data race.
elements.sent-2411-137: The address stored in \TT{counter is copied to \TT{\%3 , and this variable is used to perform the increment.}}
elements.sent-2411-138: Because of this, line \ref{codeline:race_pointer:store is not instrumented, and \tool is unable to fix the program.}
elements.sent-2411-139: Since \tool works on the LLVM IR level, such statements are split into two LLVM instructions, one for the read and another for the write.
elements.sent-2411-140: By leveraging this, \tool can fix such cases.
elements.sent-2411-141: The output message in these cases would suggest the line where the barrier needs to be inserted, but the user will have to split the line in the source program manually and insert the barrier.. \begin{minipage {.45\textwidth.}}
elements.lstlisting-2411-003: [caption=Pointer Data Race, label={Cd:race_pointer}, captionpos=b, xleftmargin=0em, escapechar=\^] static int* counter; int main() { #pragma omp parallel (*counter)++ }
elements.lstlisting-2411-004: [caption=LLVM IR, label={Cd:race_llvmir}, captionpos=b, xleftmargin=2em, escapechar=\^] store i32
elements.lstlisting-2411-005: [caption=OpenMP program with a data race in the same line, label={Cd:race_singleline}, captionpos=b, xleftmargin=2em, escapechar=\^] int data[NUM_THREADS+1]; #pragma omp parallel { int id = omp_get_thread_num(); data[id] = data[id+1]; }
elements.sent-2411-142: It can clearly be seen that the programs have a data race because of the read instruction at line \ref{codeline:llov_fortran:read and the write instruction at line \ref{codeline:llov_fortran:write .}}
elements.lstlisting-2411-006: [caption=OpenMP program in Fortran with a data race, label={Cd:llov_fortran}, captionpos=b, xleftmargin=2em, escapechar=\^] program datarace use omp_lib implicit none integer :: id, temp, threads = 10 integer :: data(11) !$omp parallel private(id,temp) id = omp_get_thread_num()+1 temp = data(id+1)^\label{codeline:llov_fortran:read}^ data(id) = temp^\label{codeline:llov_fortran:write}^ !$omp end parallel end program
elements.lstlisting-2411-007: [caption=OpenMP program in C++ with a data race, label={Cd:llov_c}, captionpos=b, xleftmargin=2em, escapechar=\^] #include "omp.h" #define NUM_THREADS 10 int main() { int data[NUM_THREADS+1]; #pragma omp parallel { int id = omp_get_thread_num(); int temp = data[id+1]; data[id] = temp; } }
elements.figure-2411-005: \caption{LLVM Instructions for the Fortran program in \coderef{llov_fortran}} \label{Ta:llvm_fortran_insts} \begin{center} \def\arraystretch{1.1} \setlength\tabcolsep{7pt} \begin{tabular}{|l|l|} \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{} \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{LLVM Instructions} 1 \makecell[l]{ \TT{\%0 = call i32 (...) @omp\_get\_thread\_num()} \TT{\%1 = add nsw i32 \%0, 1} } 2 \makecell[l]{ \TT{\%2 = sext i32 \%1 to i64} \TT{\%3 = bitcast \%struct.BSS1* @.BSS1 to i32*} \TT{\%4 = getelementptr i32, i32* \%3, i64 \%2} \TT{\%5 = load i32, i32* \%4, align 4} } 3 \makecell[l]{ \TT{\%6 = sext i32 \%1 to i64} \TT{\%7 = bitcast \%struct.BSS1* @.BSS1 to i8*} \TT{\%8 = getelementptr i8, i8* \%7, i64 -4} \TT{\%9 = bitcast i8* \%8 to i32*} \TT{\%10 = getelementptr i32, i32* \%9, i64 \%6} \TT{store i32 \%5, i32* \%10, align 4} } \end{tabular} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-143: Even though these programs are similar, \verifier is able to identify a data race in the C++ program but not in the Fortran program.
elements.sent-2411-144: Upon investigation, we found out that the LLVM IR generated by \app{Clang (an \llvm frontend for C++ programs) and \app{Flang (an \llvm frontend for Fortran programs) are different.}}
elements.sent-2411-145: There are three steps in the parallel block:.
elements.figure-2411-006: \caption{LLVM Instructions for the C++ program in \coderef{llov_c}} \label{Ta:llvm_c_insts} \begin{center} \def\arraystretch{1.1} \setlength\tabcolsep{7pt} \begin{tabular}{|l|l|} \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{} \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{LLVM Instructions} 1 \makecell[l]{ \TT{\%4 = call i32 @omp\_get\_thread\_num()} \TT{\%5 = add nsw i32 \%4, 1} } 2 \makecell[l]{ \TT{\%6 = sext i32 \%5 to i64} \TT{\%7 = getelementptr inbounds [11 x i32], [11 x i32]* \%2, i64 0, i64 \%6} \TT{\%8 = load i32, i32* \%7, align 4} } 3 \makecell[l]{ \TT{\%9 = sext i32 \%4 to i64} \TT{\%10 = getelementptr inbounds [11 x i32], [11 x i32]* \%2, i64 0, i64 \%9} \TT{store i32 \%8, i32* \%10, align 4} } \end{tabular} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-149: The LLVM IR generated by \app{Clang and \app{Flang for the first two steps are similar.}}
elements.sent-2411-150: However, the third step (writing into the \TT{data array) has a different implementation.}
elements.sent-2411-151: In the LLVM IR generated for the C++ program, the code directly accesses the memory location represented by \TT{data[omp\_get\_thread\_num()] .}
elements.sent-2411-152: In contrast, the code of the LLVM IR generated for the Fortran program first computes the memory location represented by \TT{data[omp\_get\_thread\_num()+1] and moves 4 bytes backward (since the array consists of 32-bit integers).}
elements.sent-2411-153: Cases like these reaffirm the fact that the soundness and completeness guarantees of \tool are dependent on the completeness of \verifier.
elements.lstlisting-2411-008: [caption=OpenMP program with a data race because of a write-write conflict, label={Cd:race_sameline}, captionpos=b, xleftmargin=2em, escapechar=\^] int data[NUM_THREADS+1]; #pragma omp parallel { int id = omp_get_thread_num(); data[0] = id;^\label{codeline:race_sameline:race}^ }
elements.sent-2411-155: The data race occurs because all the threads are trying to update the first location of the array data with their corresponding thread numbers.
elements.sent-2411-156: This results in a write-write conflict on the same line number.
elements.sent-2411-157: No barrier placement can mitigate the data race occurring in this program.
elements.sent-2411-158: Even though \verifier correctly identifies the data race in these programs, \tool is unable to repair them.
elements.sent-2411-159: As described in \figref{repairer_workflow , \tool uses multiple tools in the repair process.}
elements.sent-2411-160: All these steps are abstracted from the user through a single command-line utility.
elements.sent-2411-161: After installing \tool as described in \cite{llorsrc , the tool can be accessed from any folder.}
elements.sent-2411-162: Once the program is repaired, \tool prints the needed changes in the source code and provides a fixed LLVM IR program as evidence.
elements.sent-2411-163: The prerequisite for these programs is that there should be a makefile in the root folder with a target named \EM{llov .}
elements.sent-2411-164: This target should generate the LLVM IR for the various source files of the program.
elements.sent-2411-165: Examples of this makefile are provided at \cite{llorsrc .}
elements.sent-2411-166: The solver type can be changed from \mhs to \maxsat through the command-line options.
elements.sent-2411-167: Additional logging can also be enabled through these options.
elements.sent-2411-168: As mentioned in \sectref{rw_verification , several static and dynamic tools have been proposed for identifying data races in OpenMP programs.}
elements.sent-2411-169: Even though our tool uses \verifier as the verifier, in principle, the technique behind \tool works for any verifier.
elements.sent-2411-170: One possible direction that future work might take is exploring other verifiers to improve the repair coverage of \tool.
elements.sent-2411-171: In this section, we briefly introduce the other verifiers that were touched upon in \sectref{rw_verification .}
elements.sent-2411-172: The extended version of \app{PolyOMP ~\cite{chatarasi2017extended uses \EM{May-Happen-in-Parallel analysis in place of \zth.}}}
elements.sent-2411-173: It uses locksets to implement the semantic rules by taking advantage of the events tracked by OMPT.
elements.sent-2411-174: Both of them use the happens-before relations to identify data races.
elements.sent-2411-175: It uses a hybrid approach of analyzing the happens-before relations and maintaining locksets for read/write operations on shared memory.
elements.sent-2411-176: It uses \llvm to instrument the binaries instead of \app{Valgrind .}
elements.sent-2411-177: It uses happens-before relations, which enforces multiple runs of the program to find races.
